name: Rynek Underground
description: Beneath Kraków Old Town's main square lies a hologram-filled medieval market vampire graveyard wonderland.

name: Kaplica Czaszek (Chapel of Skulls)
description: The walls and ceiling of this Polish church are decorated with thousands of skulls, with another 21,000 skeletons just below.

name: Crooked Forest
description: Countless stories attempt to explain Poland's mysterious J-shaped trees.

name: Wieliczka Salt Mine
description: An underground city of salt.

name: The Bones of the Wawel Dragon
description: Real bones which are said to have belonged to a Polish dragon.

name: Wawel Dragon's Den
description: This Polish cave has housed a dragon, a brothel, and an enduring legend.

name: Ruins at Westerplatte
description: The site of the first battle of World War II.

name: The Neon Museum
description: A program of Communist glamour in post-Stalin Poland is preserved in this colorful museum.

name: Auschwitz Concentration Camp
description: The former Nazi Germany concentration camp stands as a museum to remember this dark chapter in European history.

name: Książ Castle
description: A fairytale Gothic castle steeped in Nazi treasure conspiracy theories.

name: The Wolf's Lair
description: This Nazi ruin was the site where Hitler was almost blown up by a suitcase bomb.

name: Galeria Forty / Forty
description: A hidden art gallery in an abandoned Warsaw fort.

name: Chopin's Heart
description: After the famous composer died in Paris, his sister snuck his heart out of the country to honor his wish that it be buried in their native country.

name: Wisłoujście Fortress
description: This 700-year-old structure is one of the most unique architectural and historic monuments in Gdańsk.

name: The Painted Village of Zalipie
description: This uber-quaint Polish village is known for its habit of painting elaborate floral motifs on just about everything.

name: St. Mary's Basilica
description: St. Mary's features one of the most spectacularly decorated cathedral interiors in all of Europe.

name: Collegium Maius
description: Poland's oldest surviving university building holds a wealth of historic science instruments including some used by Copernicus.

name: Warsaw Gasworks Museum
description: Warsaw's fantastically preserved gasworks...almost.

name: Kościuszko Mound
description: This man-made mound monument was built out of soil from all across the Polish empire.

name: Liban Quarry
description: The unsettling remnants of a torn down movie set lies nearby the ruined location of the actual Płaszów Labor Camp.

name: Wojtek the Soldier Bear Statue
description: A memorial to the beloved brown bear that served in the Polish army during World War II.

name: Czar PRL: Life Under Communism Museum
description: This hidden collection of everyday objects illustrates life within communist Poland.

name: Keret House
description: The world’s narrowest house makes for an awkward, four-foot-wide living space.

name: Warsaw Uprising Monument
description: A tribute to the Polish insurgents who fought in the failed attempt to end their city's Nazi occupation.

name: Okopowa Street Jewish Cemetery
description: The final resting place of more than 200,000 people, this is one of the largest cemeteries in Europe.

name: Church of St. Francis of Assisi
description: Inside this Krakow church, an explosion of psychedelic color.

name: The Warsaw Basilisk
description: A menacing statue of this mythical creature can be seen at the legendary site of the monster's lair.

name: Upside-Down House
description: Communism's topsy-turvy effect on Poland has been brought to life in this wacky tourist trap.

name: Oberża Pod Czerwonym Wieprzem
description: Order “Proletariat’s Cutlet” at this communist-themed restaurant allegedly once visited by Lenin.

name: Warsaw Poster Museum
description: A national collection of Communist-era musical posters.

name: Gdansk Astronomical Clock
description: A huge wooden clock that is so beautiful its creator was forcibly blinded.

name: Nowa Huta
description: This sprawling concrete suburb of Kraków was designed by the Soviet Union as the model proletarian city.

name: Kraków-Płaszów Concentration Camp
description: At first glance, this area seems like just another city park, but the lush landscape hides a dark past.

name: Marie Skłodowska-Curie Museum
description: This biographical museum honors the groundbreaking scientist at her birthplace in Warsaw.

name: Neon Side Gallery
description: A collection of old neon signs from all over Poland line a secluded courtyard.

name: Warsaw Fotoplastikon
description: A 20th-century picture show in 3-D.

name: Błędne Skały
description: Naturally formed rock city in Poland.

name: Moving Sand Dunes
description: These massive moving sand dunes have swallowed an entire village and are still on the run.

name: Rapa Pyramid
description: A peculiar family mausoleum built by a German nobleman captivated by the occult.

name: Łapalice Castle
description: This imposing 20th-century castle was abandoned before it was ever completed.

name: Nicolaus Copernicus House
description: Museum dedicated to "the man who moved the Earth."

name: Old Zakopane Cemetery
description: Polish luminaries and masterful folk art find sanctuary in this unique cemetery.

name: Mały Powstaniec (The Little Insurrectionist)
description: A statue honors the children who fought Nazis in Poland.

name: Holy Mountain of Grabarka
description: Crosses of all shapes, sizes and colors litter the mountain, the site of a huge yearly Orthodox pilgrimage.

name: Mermaid of Warsaw
description: The Polish capital has had a deep connection to its guardian mermaid for almost a millennium now.

name: The Exploseum
description: An abandoned Nazi explosives plant founded by the inventor of dynamite is now an underground museum.

name: Hotel Zdrowie
description: A haunting deserted hotel is a favorite with artists and photographers.

name: Świdnica Peace Church
description: This centuries-old wooden wonder was built in less than a year and is one of Europe's largest timber-framed churches.

name: Elvis Presley Avenue
description: There are thousands of tributes and memorials to the King, but none quite like this one in a Kraków city park.

name: St. Martin’s Croissant Museum
description: The traditional Polish croissant, folded 81 times into the shape of a horseshoe, is a legally protected cultural treat.

name: Gallery of Medieval Art
description: One of the world's largest collections of medieval religious art.

name: Malbork Castle
description: This 13th century Gothic brick castle is the largest in the world by surface area.

name: The Museum of Dollhouses, Games and Toys
description: A delightful museum entirely dedicated to the tiny homes of dolls.

name: Wroclaw Dwarves
description: The silly symbol for an underground protest group is now scattered around the city.

name: Przejście (Passage)
description: A striking public sculpture representing the period of martial law in Communist Poland.

name: Toruń Gingerbread Museum
description: Once a giant gingerbread factory, this space now hosts exhibits and workshops on the historic treat.

name: The Living Obwarzanek Museum (Żywe Muzeum Obwarzanka)
description: Learn the fine art of making a centuries-old treat at this hands-on food museum.

name: The Train to Heaven
description: A 90-ton, gravity-defiant steam locomotive shoots for the skies in this bizarrely beautiful public artwork.

name: Wawel Chakra
description: This grubby wall is said to be one of the Earth's esoteric energy centers.

name: Muchołapka
description: A mysterious structure left behind on the Polish mountainside, sometimes called "Hitler's Stonehenge".

name: Krzywy Domek - Crooked House
description: This fairytale inspired, warped building houses restaurants, shops, and a radio station.

name: Zofiówka Sanatorium
description: An abandoned Jewish sanatorium is tucked within the woods of Poland.

name: Bochnia Salt Mine
description: Poland's oldest salt mine has nearly everything one needs to live underground forever.

name: Marie Curie's Tree
description: A tree planted by Marie Curie is treasured by the Radium institute that she set up.

name: Gdańsk Shipyard
description: A floundering Polish shipyard was once the soul of the Solidarity movement that capsized communism.

name: Vang Stave Church
description: This fairytale medieval church in Poland originally spent centuries in Norway.

name: The Ostwall Fortification
description: What was once an underground Nazi city is now Europe's largest bat reserve.

name: Warsaw's Chopin Benches
description: Special benches that play Chopin's music are scattered throughout his native city.

name: Modlin Fortress Granary
description: In northeastern Poland, the ruins of a massive granary overlook the Narew River.

name: Torpedownia
description: A World War II torpedo launch station abandoned off the Polish coast.

name: Old Prussian Hags of Northern Pomerania
description: These rare statues are one of the few remaining material witnesses to Old Prussian culture.

name: Elbląg Canal
description: Boats must transform into railroad cars to navigate this Polish canal.

name: Niedzica Castle
description: This picturesque castle in Poland has a rich history, including a legend that ties it to lost Incan treasures.

name: The Three Boar Heads of Gdańsk
description: A mysterious emblem tucked in an small alleyway is actually the coat of arms of a heroic medieval family.

name: The Stone Circles at Odry
description: Deep in a Polish forest, this collection of Iron Age megaliths still holds onto many of its ancient secrets.

name: Nicolaus Copernicus Monument
description: The giant statue and its model solar system honor the native Pole who declared that the Earth revolves around the Sun.

name: Kraków Pinball Museum
description: A collection of modern and vintage pinball machines housed in the cellar of a 15th-century building.

name: Dwór Artusa
description: A merchant court dedicated to the fictitious King Arthur is heavily adorned with bizarre Renaissance art.

name: Hydropolis
description: This oddly beautiful water cistern is a techno-temple to the wet stuff and a multimedia masterpiece.

name: Mieszko the Stone Bear
description: Legend says this is actually a heartbroken prince waiting for his true love's kiss.

name: Cemetery of Lost Cemeteries
description: Memorial to the lost dead of Gdansk, whose resting places were destroyed during World War II.

name: Old Town Wishing Bell
description: This bell stars in a tragic love tale and is said to make wishes come true.

name: Fahrenheit Monument
description: This antique thermometer honors the creator of the first universal temperature scale.

name: Lord's Ark
description: In the midst of a living monument to Soviet social engineering, Polish Catholics built this church by hand.

name: St. Annaberg Thingstätte
description: One of the largest amphitheaters in Europe is a former Nazi German gathering place that still stands today.

name: Oak Jozef
description: The hollow trunk of this 650-year-old tree was used as a hideout by two Jewish brothers during World War II.

name: Zakłady Włókien Chemicznych
description: Dubbed "Wistom" by locals, this abandoned factory is an urban explorer's dream.

name: Battle of Grunwald Painting
description: This enormous 32-foot painting depicts Poland's greatest medieval battle in glorious detail.

name: Warsaw University Library Garden
description: Atop this library is one of the most beautiful green spaces in Warsaw.

name: Dżok the Dog Monument
description: A statue commemorating a Polish dog who took his role as man's best friend very seriously.

name: Zabytkowa Dzwonnica (Cloud Demon Repelling Tower)
description: This tower in a small Polish village uses the power of a sacred bell to ward off mischievous weather spirits.

name: The Fighting Goats of Poznań
description: These mechanical goats first battled it out in the year 1551, all because of an overcooked deer.

name: Nazi Rocket Test Site
description: This open-air museum was originally a World War II testing ground for German missiles and rockets.

name: The Canaletto Room
description: These 18th-century paintings of Warsaw were used to reconstruct the city after World War II.

name: Stalag Luft III Prisoner Camp Museum
description: The site of one of the largest prison escapes of World War II.

name: Chopin Monument
description: In the summer, the area around this sculpture transforms into a vibrant concert venue.

name: The Wagrowiec Bifurcation
description: One of only two places in the world where two rivers cross each other without the waters mixing.

name: Shrine of St. Thaddaeus
description: Hundreds of notes give thanks to the patron saint of lost causes.

name: Monument In Honor of the Slaughtered Animals
description: In the center of Wrocław's old meat market stands a somber tribute to the city's livestock.

name: Falowiec
description: This Communist-era residential block is one of the longest residential building in Europe.

name: Totenburg Mausoleum
description: This mysterious and once-grand memorial still embodies a remarkable architectural vision.

name: Museum of Soap and History of Dirt
description: Come to this Polish museum to take a good, clean look at filth.

name: Emilcin UFO memorial
description: A monument to Poland's famous alleged UFO abduction case.

name: Monument to the Defenders of the Polish Post Office
description: Modern art commemorates one of the first military actions of World War II.

name: Muzeum Zabawek (Toy Museum)
description: The largest toy museum in Poland showcases Europe's history of childhood.

name: Carthusian Church Monastery
description: The coffin-shaped church was once home to monks who slept in coffins.

name: Spodek
description: While the U.S. and Soviet Union were engaged in a space race, Poland built a UFO on the ground.

name: Trójstyk Granic
description: A small granite monument marks where the borders of three countries meet.

name: Karpacz's Gravitational Anomaly
description: On this Polish road, an optical illusion makes cars seem to roll uphill.

name: Muzeum Treblinka
description: A somber memorial honors the hundreds of thousands of lives lost at this labor and extermination camp.

name: Edmund Szyc Stadium
description: An abandoned football stadium with a tragic past.

name: Racławice Panorama
description: Relive a Polish uprising via an enormous, century-old cyclorama.

name: Sadpol Strawberry
description: The giant metal fruit was once the symbol of a large farm.

name: Stańczyki Viaducts
description: The two abandoned overpasses are among the largest bridges in Poland.

name: Leśniewo U-Boat Locks
description: Built to transport German U-boats through the never-completed Masurian Canal, the locks near this Polish village are now home to a rope course.

name: The World's Tallest Pope Statue
description: Brought to you by the makers of fiberglass dinosaurs.

name: Museum of Folk Architecture
description: An open-air museum of 18th-century homes and churches transported from various villages in Southern Poland.

name: Warsaw Pegasus Sculptures
description: This colorful and quirky set of pegasus sculptures celebrate the works of poet Zbigniew Herbert.

name: Kopiec Wandy (Wanda Mound)
description: An artificial hill and apparent calendric structure built in the Early Middle Ages immortalizes a legendary Polish princess.

name: Nuclear Bunkers of Nowa Huta
description: An underground city beneath Krakow's postwar socialist utopia.

name: Internet Antennas of Christ the King
description: This towering statue of Jesus provided internet to the surrounding area.

name: The Land of Open Shutters
description: Old cabin-like homes in a rural community untouched by time are known for their vibrant and colorful window shutters.

name: Bismarck Tower Ruin
description: This hilltop ruin is of the hundreds of stone columns built in tribute to the first German chancellor.

name: Grave of Rudolf Kwiek
description: The final resting place of the last "gypsy king" of Poland.

name: Błędów Desert
description: People in Poland have rallied to save this unnatural desert from being reclaimed by the forest.

name: The Dental Museum and Mummy of Racibórz
description: The biggest exposition of dental equipement in Europe. And a Mummy.

name: Kraków-Płaszów Train Graveyard
description: This cemetery of rusting Soviet-era steam locomotives and train cars offers an apocalyptic glimpse into a bygone era.

name: St. Bridget's Church
description: One of the world's largest amber altarpieces is housed within this reconstructed medieval church.

name: Nierozpoznani
description: A cluster of headless, armless sculptures looms within a popular Polish park.

name: Tower Clock Museum
description: This museum in the tower of the oldest church in Gdańsk, is home to centuries-old time-keeping devices and the world's first pulsar clock.

name: Rysy
description: The majority of the highest mountain in Poland is actually located in a different country.

name: Abecadło
description: A 19th-century pharmacy converted into a bookstore.

name: Jomsborg Marker
description: A stone in Wolin, Poland, marks the possible location of a legendary Viking fortress.

name: St John’s Arch Cathedral
description: The last king of Poland was interred in this church nearly 200 years after his death.

name: Muzeum Gier Wideo (Krakow Arcade Museum)
description: Where visitors can play more than 150 retro arcade games.

name: Statue of Jan Matejko
description: Extremely famous in his native country, this Cracovian painter is all but unknown beyond Polish borders.

name: Wikipedia Monument
description: The first, and perhaps only, monument dedicated to Wikipedia.

name: Memorial to the Victims of Air Pollution
description: Down this Cracovian street resides a plaque with a stark message about the city's air pollution.

name: The Monument Tree of Pawiak
description: A bronze replica of an elm tree that honors the victims that perished in Pawaik Prison.

name: Podziemne Miasto Osówka
description: An extensive network of secret tunnels was created for the Nazi project "Riese."

name: 'Greetings from Jerusalem Avenue'
description: An artificial date palm stands as a memorial to Warsaw's Jewish community.

name: The Blue Palace
description: Once home to some 250,000 volumes and priceless art artifacts, this Polish estate was a target of bombing during World War II.

name: Wrocław’s Former City Prison
description: This ex-prison dates back to the Middle Ages.

name: 'A Footbridge of Memory'
description: At night, this memorial illuminates a ghostly outline of a footbridge that connected two sections of the Warsaw Ghetto.

name: Rębielice Królewskie Wind Turbine
description: The oldest wind turbine in Europe, constructed by a group of friends in 1932.

name: Sochaczew Narrow Gauge Railway Museum
description: This unique railroad museum secretly contains the history of Poland rebuilding itself from the inside-out.

name: Rondo Wolnego Tybetu (Free Tibet Roundabout)
description: A heavily politicized roundabout discussing the state of a heavily politicized country.

name: The Dragon Trail
description: Seven dragon statues—including one that breathes real fire—are scattered throughout the streets of Krakow.

name: Pieces of St. Valentine's Skull
description: The relics are stored in a silver reliquary, where they can be seen through glass.

name: Mazurolandia
description: A miniatures park with a model of Wolf's Lair as it looked during World War II.

name: Gliwice Radio Tower
description: Gliwice Radio Tower, likely the tallest wooden structure in the world.

name: The Chopin Centre in Szafarnia
description: The manor house where the Polish composer spent childhood summer holidays is now a museum and concert hall.

name: Warsaw Radio Mast Remains
description: The shattered remains of the world's once tallest structure.

name: Tchorek Plaques
description: These plaques found across Warsaw commemorate the sites where executions or battles took place during the German occupation.

name: Mauerwald Bunker Complex
description: This lakeside bunker complex in Mamerki, Poland, housed the Supreme Military Command of Nazi Germany during World War II.

name: Poznan June 1956 Monument
description: An impressive monument to Poland's independence.

name: Uniwersytecki Bridge Hand
description: A mysterious concrete hand reaches out from between the stones of Uniwersytecki bridge.

name: National Museum of Technology in Warsaw
description: This museum houses and extensive collection of technology contributed by the Polish people.

name: Gnojna Góra (Dung Hill Lookout Point)
description: Catch a scenic view of Warsaw's Old Town from the top of a centuries-old dumping ground.

name: The Secret Polish Eagle on Fara Church
description: This symbol of Polish heritage survived the occupation during World War II, hidden in plain sight.

name: Szczepan Mucha's Wooden Sculptures
description: Disturbing works of outsider art, carved from whole logs.

name: Krakow Torch Sockets
description: Remnants from a time before electricity.

name: The Disappeared City of Miedzianka
description: This formerly-prosperous Silesian mining town was completely demolished in the late 1960s and early 1970s as an attempt to cover up a secret Soviet uranium mine.

name: Bemisie
description: A group of tiny bears has occupied various spots across Warsaw's Bemowo district.

name: Skrzyńsko Parish Church
description: Legend says a giant, venomous spider once dwelled within this Polish church.

name: Grzyb skalny w Zegartowice (Zegartowice Mushroom Rock)
description: This mushroom-shaped rock has frustrated and fascinated locals for centuries.

name: Aleja Gwiazd (Avenue of Stars)
description: Krakow's own version of the Hollywood Walk of Fame.

name: Jawor Peace Church
description: This dazzling wooden church was constructed as part of an elaborate peace project after the Thirty Years' War.

name: The Disappeared City Center of Kostrzyn
description: The center of this Polish border town was so thoroughly wiped out in World War II that only the foundations remain.

name: Wieżowiec-Zamek (Castle Skyscraper)
description: A Soviet era flat that refused to conform to the dull gray standards.

name: 'Pracownia (Studio) Forresta Bessa'
description: A marker honors the brief peace found by a tormented artist in the fictional land of Kcymaerxthaere.

name: Fort Sarbinowo
description: This abandoned but well-preserved Prussian fortress sits abandoned in a Polish forest near the German border.

name: The Cloth Hall Knife
description: Legend holds that this knife serves as a chilling reminder of the deadly rivalry between two local builder brothers.

name: The Mammoth Bone
description: A massive mammoth bone has been on display inside this church since the 18th century.

name: Beached Wreck of the Warship Grom II
description: The rusting hulk of a Soviet warship sits on a Polish beach.

name: Archaeological Remains at Hala Targowa (Market Hall)
description: Several 12th-century ruins can be found inside this historical market.

name: Pomnik Jurija Gagarina w Poznaniu
description: A tribute to the first human in space.

name: Lunar Rover at Mariacka Street
description: During the renovation of this bas-relief, the constructors added one unusual element.

name: Medieval Execution Pole
description: This 16th-century pole was once used in brutal public executions.

name: Museum of Stanisław Wyspiański
description: Beautiful portraits of the artist's children and his amazing stained glass windows in the Art Nouveau style.

name: Walter Gropius's 1906 Spichlerz
description: A Bauhaus co-founder designed this quirky, abandoned granary.

name: Leśna Galeria (Forest Gallery)
description: A small square populated with giant birchwood sculptures.

name: Mermaid of Ustka
description: This Baltic beauty is rumored to grant a wish and a half.

name: Besiekiery Castle Ruins
description: These beautiful castle ruins are nestled in the Polish countryside.

name: Green Mosque of Kruszyniany
description: This wooden mosque is one of the few remnants of medieval Poland's Muslim Tatar community in this deeply Catholic country.

name: Metroteka
description: This alluring underground library, hidden in a suburban Metro station, features a Space Age aesthetic and its own hydroponic garden.

name: Gdański Lew Hewelion
description: Gdańsk's lion mascot features in a trail of bronze sculptures scattered all across the city.

name: Pustynia Kozłowska
description: How did this desert wind up in the middle of a Polish forest?

name: The Versailles Border Stone
description: The original stone marking the interwar border between Germany and Poland sits in this unassuming village park.

name: Crane M3
description: A relic of Poland's shipbuilding past, reborn as a sky-high lookout.

name: Church of Our Lady Queen of Poland
description: A striking church with a Tolkien-inspired nickname.

name: Pilchowice Hydroelectic Dam
description: The tallest stone dam in Poland was opened by Kaiser Wilhelm II himself.

name: Kielce Boar Monument
description: A large boar commemorates the city's founding and the origin of its name.

name: Ciechocinek Salt Graduation Tower
description: For 200 years, people have flocked to this Polish town to experience the healing powers of salt.

name: Pinball Station
description: This interactive museum celebrates the iconic arcade game.

name: The Witches’ Tower
description: According to local legend, this unassuming art gallery was once used to imprison witches.

name: Slik House Weichmann
description: An early creation by Erich Mendelsohn.

name: Galleria EL
description: This bombed church was given a new life as a contemporary art gallery.

name: King Eric's Golden Dove
description: This statue is said to bring good luck to visiting lovebirds.

name: Elektrownia Powiśle
description: One of Warsaw's coolest malls was once a center for anti-Nazi resistance activity.

name: Fangor Mosaics
description: Complex tile installations designed by a renowned Polish artist in a Warsaw subway.

name: Rowokół
description: For centuries, this hill along the Polish coast has been used as a lookout—and the rumored home of pirate treasure.

name: Signpost Park
description: This public park collects signposts and other memorabilia related to local history, Polish-German relations and human progress

name: Kwidzyn Dansker
description: Prior to modern plumbing, some toilets were in tall towers perched over rivers.

name: The Illusory Window
description: It’s a painted window that looks completely real, hiding on a building in the market square.

name: The Blast Furnace of Huta Pokój
description: A towering relic of Upper Silesia’s industrial past, the blast furnace of Huta Pokój stands as a monumental reminder of the region’s former role as one of Central Europe’s most important iron and steel production centers.

name: Cemetery-Monument in Wawer
description: A symbolic cemetery in a suburban square marks the birthplace of Poland's WWII resistance movement.

name: Konin Milepost
description: Oldest surviving milestone in Europe outside of the Roman Empire.

name: Monument to the Polish Victory at Cedynia
description: An installation at the border commemorates a battle from 972.

name: Faculty of Architecture Building
description: An early 20th-century school building whose clock tower and carved symbols quietly tell the story of the trades once taught inside.

name: Wałśniak Oak
description: This ancient tree in the middle of a street united the community in an effort to preserve it.

name: Polish Army Museum
description: This extensive collection covers thousands of years of Polish military history.

name: Skarpa Ski Jump
description: Ruins of a late 1950s Polish ski jump ramp in the middle of the city.

name: Hydrierwerke Pölitz
description: This now-defunct synthetic fuel plant was once a major cog in the Nazi war machine.
